refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability when faults occur.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to eliminate central points of failure and enable partial operation.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) system with automated diagnostics and operator alerting to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP7: Combine redundancy (DP1), modularity (DP2), and monitoring (DP3, DP6) in an integrated availability architecture with defined recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP7: Integrated availability architecture combining (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) modular hot-swappable design for rapid component replacement, (3) predictive condition monitoring to detect degradation early, and (4) comprehensive fault detection and isolation with automated diagnostics—all coordinated to achieve target availability ≥0.97 by minimizing both failure frequency and mean-time-to-repair.

potential_sources: Redundancy strategy (DP1 element) must be coordinated with failover logic timing to avoid cascading failures during switchover.
Modularity boundaries (DP2 element) must align with redundancy domains to ensure swappable units are truly independent.
Condition monitoring (DP3 element) must feed into FDIA (DP6 element) to avoid duplicate sensing or conflicting diagnostics.
Graceful degradation thresholds (DP4 element) must be defined consistently with RTO/RPO targets to prevent uncontrolled service loss.
Distributed control autonomy (DP5 element) must synchronize with centralized monitoring (DP6 element) without creating race conditions or information delays.
coupling_check.notes: DP7 is a composite design that integrates multiple logical strategies. Coupling risk is moderate if boundaries between redundancy domains, modularity, monitoring, and control are clearly defined in the IM layer. Recommend explicit interface contracts and state synchronization protocols to decouple these elements.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be procured, integrated, and maintained within cost and schedule constraints. Risk: Over-specification of redundancy may exceed budget; under-specification may fail to meet availability target.
Assumption: Predictive maintenance models are accurate and calibrated for the operational environment. Risk: Model drift or environmental variability may reduce early-warning effectiveness.
Assumption: Automatic failover logic is deterministic and tested exhaustively. Risk: Failover bugs or race conditions could cause longer outages than manual intervention.

Assumption: MTTR can be reduced to acceptable levels through modularity and diagnostics. Risk: Supply chain delays, technician availability, or physical access constraints may prevent rapid replacement.
Assumption: Distributed control can maintain consistency without a central authority. Risk: Network partitions or clock skew may cause split-brain scenarios or data inconsistency.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies support automated failover and predictive maintenance workflows. Risk: Regulatory or procedural constraints may require manual approval, increasing effective MTTR.
Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough and representative enough to validate ≥0.97 availability claim. Risk: Short or atypical evaluation windows may not capture rare failure modes.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) definition of 'operational availability' (e.g., uptime percentage, mean-time-between-failures, mean-time-to-repair targets), (2) scope of 'critical functions' vs. 'non-critical functions' for graceful degradation, (3) acceptable RTO and RPO for each subsystem, (4) constraints on redundancy cost and complexity, and (5) assumptions about technician response time and spare parts availability. This will enable tighter DP-to-IM mapping and reduce ambiguity in acceptance testing. Additionally, confirm that the worksite safety plan and operational policies explicitly permit automated failover and predictive maintenance actions without manual intervention delays.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated availability architecture encompassing redundancy coordination, hot-swappable component management, predictive condition monitoring, and fault detection/isolation across all subsystems within the NGHE platform

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound health and performance data from Vehicle subsystems, Teleoperation systems, and Safety Services to feed predictive monitoring and fault detection

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound commands to Vehicle and Teleoperation for failover activation, component replacement coordination, and automated diagnostics execution

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound critical availability and fault alerts to Safety Services when degradation or failures are detected

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication for distributed redundancy coordination and remote diagnostics across geographically dispersed subsystems

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the platform-level integrator responsible for cross-subsystem availability orchestration. Redundancy coordination, hot-swap management, predictive monitoring, and fault isolation all require system-wide visibility and control. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement specific redundancy patterns and expose health telemetry, but core availability logic resides in NGHE.

Poor containment: Vehicle is a subsystem, not a platform integrator. Redundancy, predictive monitoring, and fault isolation require coordination across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Placing availability architecture here would fragment logic and create tight coupling to Vehicle internals. High diffusion: failover logic, diagnostics, and monitoring would leak into Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a subsystem focused on remote control and situational awareness, not system-wide availability. Predictive monitoring and fault isolation require access to Vehicle and Safety Services state. Placing availability architecture here creates artificial coupling and violates separation of concerns. High diffusion: redundancy and diagnostics logic would scatter across Vehicle and Safety Services.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a specialized subsystem for safety-critical alerts and compliance, not a general availability orchestrator. Redundancy management, hot-swap coordination, and predictive monitoring are orthogonal to safety-specific functions. Placing availability architecture here conflates safety with availability and creates high coupling to Vehicle and Teleoperation internals. High diffusion: failover and diagnostics would diffuse widely.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP7 is a platform-level availability strategy that requires system-wide coordination across all subsystems. NGHE Solution, as the integrating platform, is the natural owner. It has visibility into Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services health; it can orchestrate failover and hot-swap events; it can aggregate telemetry for predictive monitoring; and it can execute distributed fault detection and diagnostics. Containment is strong because availability is a cross-cutting concern that cannot be localized to any single subsystem without creating fragmentation and coupling.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems implement their own redundancy or fault detection independently, NGHE Solution's coordinated availability logic may be undermined or duplicated. Mitigation: Establish clear contracts that Vehicle and Teleoperation expose health telemetry and accept failover/diagnostics commands from NGHE, but do not implement independent availability strategies.

Risk: Predictive condition monitoring requires deep knowledge of subsystem degradation patterns. If NGHE Solution lacks sufficient telemetry granularity from Vehicle or Teleoperation, monitoring accuracy will degrade. Mitigation: Define telemetry schema and sampling rates in collaboration with subsystem owners to ensure NGHE receives sufficient signal for early degradation detection.

Risk: Hot-swappable component design requires Vehicle and Teleoperation to implement modular interfaces. If subsystems are tightly coupled internally, NGHE's hot-swap coordination will fail. Mitigation: Enforce modular design principles in Vehicle and Teleoperation as a prerequisite for availability architecture.

Risk: Network Link availability is critical for distributed redundancy and remote diagnostics. If Network Link fails, NGHE's coordinated availability strategy may collapse. Mitigation: Design NGHE to degrade gracefully to local-only failover and diagnostics when Network Link is unavailable.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface must be reliable and low-latency. If NGHE Solution's fault detection is slow or unreliable, Safety Services may not receive timely alerts. Mitigation: Implement SafetyAlert as a high-priority, asynchronous channel with guaranteed delivery semantics.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP7 is well-formulated and appropriately scoped to the platform level. No revision is needed. The DP correctly identifies availability as an integrated, cross-cutting concern that must be orchestrated at the NGHE Solution level. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) should implement the specific redundancy patterns, modular interfaces, and telemetry exports that NGHE requires, but the overall availability architecture and coordination logic must reside in NGHE Solution to avoid fragmentation and ensure coherent failover and diagnostics.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability ≥0.97 is measurable and verifiable over the agreed evaluation period through operational telemetry aggregation at the NGHE Solution level. Metrics include: (1) cumulative uptime across all subsystems, (2) failover activation count and success rate, (3) mean-time-to-repair (MTTR) reduction via hot-swap and automated diagnostics, (4) predictive monitoring accuracy (false positive/negative rates). NGHE Solution's central telemetry ingestion and logging provide the observability foundation required for gate verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns the integrated availability architecture and is accountable for achieving the 0.97 target. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are allocated specific responsibilities: Vehicle and Teleoperation must expose health telemetry and support hot-swappable modular design; Safety Services must receive and act on SafetyAlert notifications. Network Link must provide reliable bidirectional communication for distributed coordination. Ownership boundaries are explicit and non-overlapping.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP7 directly and sufficiently addresses FR (operational availability ≥0.97). The four pillars—redundancy with automatic failover, modular hot-swappable design, predictive condition monitoring, and comprehensive fault detection/isolation—are logically sound and collectively target both failure frequency reduction (via redundancy and predictive monitoring) and MTTR reduction (via hot-swap and automated diagnostics). The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific technologies) and is appropriately scoped to the platform level. Acceptance criterion (≥0.97 availability) is directly traceable to DP success metrics.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems may independently implement redundancy or fault detection logic, creating duplicate availability strategies and undermining NGHE's coordinated orchestration. Mitigation: Establish explicit contracts requiring subsystems to expose health telemetry and accept failover/diagnostics commands from NGHE only; prohibit independent availability logic.

Risk: Predictive condition monitoring depends on telemetry granularity and sampling rates from Vehicle and Teleoperation. Insufficient signal fidelity will degrade early degradation detection accuracy. Mitigation: Co-define telemetry schema, sampling rates, and data quality SLAs with subsystem owners before implementation.

Risk: Hot-swappable component design requires Vehicle and Teleoperation to implement modular internal interfaces. If subsystems are tightly coupled, NGHE's hot-swap coordination will fail or be delayed. Mitigation: Enforce modular design principles and interface contracts in subsystem architecture reviews.

Risk: Network Link unavailability will break distributed redundancy coordination and remote diagnostics. NGHE's availability strategy may collapse if Network Link is not sufficiently resilient. Mitigation: Design NGHE to degrade gracefully to local-only failover and diagnostics; establish Network Link SLA (e.g., ≥0.99 availability) as a prerequisite.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface reliability and latency are critical. Slow or unreliable fault detection will delay safety notifications. Mitigation: Implement SafetyAlert as a high-priority, asynchronous channel with guaranteed delivery semantics and <100ms latency target.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Containment score of 0.92 for NGHE Solution is justified. DP7 is a platform-level, cross-cutting concern that cannot be localized to any single subsystem without fragmentation. NGHE Solution, as the integrating platform, has system-wide visibility and control required for coordinated redundancy, hot-swap management, predictive monitoring, and fault isolation. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and expected: subsystems must implement specific redundancy patterns and expose telemetry, but core availability orchestration logic resides in NGHE. Alternative allocations (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) all score poorly (0.35–0.45 containment, 0.55–0.65 diffusion) because they lack cross-subsystem visibility and would create artificial coupling. Current allocation is optimal.

recommended_revisions: No revision to DP7 is required. The DP is well-formulated, appropriately scoped, and correctly allocated to NGHE Solution.

Strengthen containment by formalizing subsystem contracts: (1) Vehicle and Teleoperation must define telemetry export interfaces (schema, sampling rates, data quality SLAs) in collaboration with NGHE. (2) Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement modular hot-swappable component interfaces per NGHE specifications. (3) Vehicle and Teleoperation must accept failover and diagnostics commands from NGHE and must not implement independent availability logic.
Establish Network Link SLA (e.g., ≥0.99 availability) as a prerequisite for NGHE's coordinated availability strategy. Document graceful degradation behavior when Network Link is unavailable.

Define SafetyAlert interface contract: high-priority, asynchronous, guaranteed delivery, <100ms latency target. Implement as a separate, dedicated channel to avoid coupling with other NGHE interfaces.
Add acceptance criterion: Predictive monitoring accuracy (false positive rate <5%, false negative rate <2%) measured over the evaluation period to ensure early degradation detection is reliable.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. DP7 is logically sound, correctly allocated to NGHE Solution, and verification is feasible. However, the gate analysis reveals critical diffusion risks and implementation dependencies that cannot be resolved at the current level of abstraction. Specifically: (1) subsystem contracts for telemetry export, modular design, and failover command acceptance are prerequisites but not yet formalized; (2) Network Link SLA and graceful degradation behavior are unspecified; (3) SafetyAlert interface contract (latency, delivery semantics) is critical for safety but not yet detailed; (4) predictive monitoring accuracy targets are recommended but not yet decomposed into sub-requirements. These are not defects in DP7 itself, but rather dependencies that must be resolved through next-level decomposition to ensure implementation feasibility and risk containment. Proceeding to sub-FRs will expose and formalize these dependencies, enabling subsystem teams to execute with clear contracts and measurable acceptance criteria.
next_level_sub_frs: FR7.1: Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation) shall expose standardized health telemetry (schema, sampling rates, data quality SLAs) to NGHE Solution for predictive condition monitoring and coordinated failover orchestration.
FR7.2: Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation) shall implement modular, hot-swappable component interfaces per NGHE specifications to enable rapid component replacement without system shutdown.

FR7.3: Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation) shall accept and execute failover and diagnostics commands from NGHE Solution and shall not implement independent availability logic that conflicts with NGHE's coordinated orchestration.
FR7.4: Network Link shall provide reliable bidirectional communication with ≥0.99 availability to support distributed redundancy coordination, remote diagnostics, and SafetyAlert delivery.
FR7.5: NGHE Solution shall implement graceful degradation to local-only failover and diagnostics when Network Link is unavailable, maintaining minimum operational availability ≥0.95 in degraded mode.
FR7.6: Safety Services shall receive and act on SafetyAlert notifications from NGHE Solution with guaranteed delivery semantics and <100ms latency to ensure timely safety intervention.
FR7.7: NGHE Solution's predictive condition monitoring shall achieve false positive rate <5% and false negative rate <2% over the evaluation period to ensure reliable early degradation detection.

revise.why: No revision to FR, DP, or IM is required at the current level. DP7 is well-formulated and correctly allocated. Revisions recommended by the gate analysis (subsystem contracts, Network Link SLA, SafetyAlert interface, predictive monitoring accuracy targets) are best addressed through next-level sub-FR decomposition rather than rework of the current level.

assumptions: Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation) are capable of implementing modular, hot-swappable component interfaces and exposing standardized telemetry without major architectural rework.
Network Link can be designed or upgraded to achieve ≥0.99 availability; if not feasible, NGHE's availability target may need to be revised downward.
Predictive condition monitoring algorithms can be tuned to achieve <5% false positive and <2% false negative rates with the telemetry fidelity available from subsystems.

Safety Services can be integrated to receive and act on SafetyAlert notifications with <100ms latency; if not feasible, safety-critical failover logic may need to be localized to Vehicle or Teleoperation.
NGHE Solution has sufficient computational and storage capacity to ingest, process, and store telemetry from all subsystems at the required sampling rates without introducing latency or data loss.
open_risks: Subsystem teams may resist implementing modular interfaces or exposing detailed telemetry due to perceived complexity or performance overhead. Mitigation: Establish executive sponsorship and clear business case for availability improvements; provide reference implementations and tooling.

Telemetry schema and sampling rates may diverge across subsystems, creating data integration burden for NGHE. Mitigation: Establish a cross-functional telemetry working group early in design phase to co-define schema, sampling rates, and data quality SLAs.

Network Link SLA of ≥0.99 availability may be unachievable with current infrastructure (e.g., single ISP, single data center). Mitigation: Conduct network architecture review; consider multi-path routing, edge caching, or local failover if ≥0.99 is infeasible.

SafetyAlert latency target of <100ms may be difficult to achieve if Safety Services is geographically distributed or if NGHE's processing pipeline introduces queuing delays. Mitigation: Implement SafetyAlert as a dedicated, high-priority channel with priority queuing and latency monitoring.

Predictive monitoring accuracy may degrade over time due to model drift or changing operational conditions. Mitigation: Establish continuous model retraining and validation process; monitor false positive/negative rates in production and adjust thresholds as needed.

Graceful degradation to local-only failover may not be feasible if Vehicle and Teleoperation lack sufficient local redundancy or decision-making logic. Mitigation: Conduct feasibility study on local failover capabilities; may require additional investment in subsystem-level redundancy.
evidence_needed: Subsystem architecture reviews confirming modular, hot-swappable component design is feasible and planned.
Telemetry schema and sampling rate specifications co-defined by NGHE, Vehicle, and Teleoperation teams.
Network Link architecture design and SLA commitment (≥0.99 availability) from network/infrastructure team.
SafetyAlert interface specification (schema, delivery semantics, latency target) and integration plan with Safety Services.
Predictive monitoring algorithm design and validation results showing false positive/negative rates <5% and <2% respectively on representative data.
Graceful degradation design and testing results confirming minimum ≥0.95 availability in local-only failover mode.
Acceptance test plan and success criteria for each sub-FR, with clear ownership and measurement methodology.
